lines.text: Trios for the Ridgemont History Fair. No trading, no negotiating — I've seen what happens when I allow negotiating.
A ripple of groans and relief moves through the room as names get read off.
Nico — you're with Jordan and Maya.
Yes. Okay. This is gonna be good.
Hi. I already started a source list. Just so it exists somewhere.
lines.action: doesn't look up from her notecards

lines.text: Cool. Uh — cool. That's... a lot of colors.
They're organized by era. It's not a big deal.
Good luck. You're gonna need it.
Wait, why do you say it like that's a threat?
Because it kind of is.
lines.action: already turning back to their own group

lines.text: Don't listen to Casey. We're gonna crush this.
New group. New rules. Please don't let this be the project that ends a friendship before it even starts.
Library Study Room B. Jordan's got a marker uncapped before anyone's even sat down.
Okay okay, hear me out — what if the whole board is just, like, one big timeline, but it pops, you know? Pull-tabs. Hidden facts. Very interactive.
Pull-tabs. On a poster board.
You're overthinking the delivery. Trust the vision.
I actually found some primary sources from the historical society archive — letters, mostly. If we built the whole thing around those instead of—
lines.action: opens her folder, notecards already squared

lines.text: Right, right, sources, we'll throw some in. But the pop-up thing is the hook. That's what gets the grade.

lines.action: stops mid-sentence, goes very still, pulls her folder a little closer against her chest

lines.text: She didn't even get to finish the sentence.
Jordan's kitchen table. Two days out. A half-finished poster board leans against a cereal box nobody's put away.
Okay so here's the thing. My brother did this exact project like three years ago. Same rubric, basically. I found the file.
This is... a whole project. Formatted and everything.
Right? We just gotta swap some facts, change the images, boom. Done tonight instead of Thursday at midnight.
Wait — so we'd just be using his old one? Like, structurally?
It's basically a template at this point. Don't overthink it. We've got zero time and this saves us, like, six hours.
I already have most of the research done. It's just not typed up yet.
lines.action: squaring notecards against the table edge

lines.text: Which is great, we can drop it right into this. Everybody wins.
The template's closed. Somewhere past ten, the kitchen table is covered in notecards and typed pages.
Okay, remind me why we didn't just—
Because then it's not actually ours.
Yeah, yeah. Noted.
Okay, so — this letter is actually the good part. The mill owner wrote to his brother complaining about the workers unionizing, and it's dated two weeks before the strike actually happened.
Wait, that's — huh. That's actually a pretty wild detail.
I know. I've been sitting on it for a week and nobody asked.
Okay, well. I'm asking now.
First time all week Jordan's actually looked at one of her notecards.
There's more where that came from.
lines.action: taps her pencil twice, starts writing

lines.text: The template fills in fast — a name here, a date there. It looks good. It looks done.
See? Told you. Basically a project in a box.
I can swap in the sources I found instead of his old ones. It'll still be quick.
Yeah, sure, whatever's fastest.
This feels... weirdly easy.
That's called doing it right. Come on.
lines.action: holds up a hand for a high-five
returns the high-five, a beat too slow

lines.text: It's fine. I'll just, um — fix the formatting so it doesn't look like his brother's colors.
It looked done. It didn't feel done. Not sure those are the same thing.
They keep the template's bones — the layout, the timeline — but everything else gets checked, sourced, rebuilt.
This is taking so much longer than it needed to.
It's taking exactly as long as it needs to.
This source is wrong, by the way. His brother cited the wrong treaty year. Good thing we're checking.
...Okay, that's actually a good catch.
It's not a big deal. I just read footnotes.
Slower. Messier. But it's starting to look like something we actually made.
Fine. Fine! We cite everything. Happy?
Getting there.
Jordan's at the printer down the hall. Maya squares her notecards against the table edge, not looking up.
Can I say something and have it not be a whole thing?
Yeah. Of course.
I did most of the research. Like, almost all of it. The organizing, the sourcing, the timeline logic. I'm not saying that to complain.
I mean — I know. I've seen the notecards.
Right. The notecards.
I just don't know if my name's actually going to be on the slide. Jordan's been kind of... running the show.
Has he said your name wouldn't be on it?
No. He hasn't said anything. That's kind of the problem.
I'm not trying to make it a whole thing. I just did most of it, and I guess I wanted someone to know that.
The three of them are back around the laptop, the credits slide open.
Hey, Jordan — can we talk about the credits slide for a sec?
What about it?
It's got your name huge at the top and Maya's kind of... small, at the bottom.
I mean — it's not like I said it wasn't a group thing. I just — it's alphabetical. Kind of.
Jordan. She did most of the research.
...Yeah. No, you're right. That was — yeah. My bad.
Okay. There. Same size. Both names first line.
Thank you.
Don't make it weird. We're still a team.
We're still a team.
I think it'll work out. Try not to stress about it too much.
Yeah. Probably.
I'll just, um — keep formatting.
That didn't feel like an answer. Felt more like the end of a conversation nobody actually finished.
It's fine.
I think this needs to go to Ms. Patel. Will you come with me?
Like — now? Before presentation day?
Yeah. Before, not after.
...Okay. Yeah. I don't want to do this by myself, so — okay.
lines.action: long pause, then a small nod

lines.text: They find Ms. Patel after school, the classroom quiet except for a humming radiator.
This sounds like it's going to be a real conversation. Sit.
lines.action: takes off her glasses, cleans them

lines.text: I did most of the research. Jordan's name was going to be the only one people really noticed. And we — we used part of his brother's old project as a starting template.
lines.action: squares her notecards even though there's no table edge nearby

lines.text: We fixed the sourcing, mostly. But it started as a shortcut. I should've said something sooner.
I appreciate that. Both of you. Coming to me now instead of Friday? That's not nothing.
So we're not — this isn't going to blow up the whole project?
I'll grade with full information. That's all that means — full information. Go on, you'll be late for lunch.
The projector hums. Jordan's in front, riding the momentum of his own voice.
So basically, I built this whole timeline concept, and — yeah, I did a lot of the research myself, digging through the archive stuff—

lines.action: goes small and still, eyes down at her own slide

lines.text: From the back row, Casey catches the player's eye — a raised eyebrow, a question with no words.
—and that's kind of the throughline of the whole project, honestly.
The projector hums. Whatever Ms. Patel said to Jordan after school, it stuck.
So — a lot of this research, especially the primary sources, is actually Maya's. I want to be clear about that up front.
lines.emotion: a little stiff, reading off his own notes

lines.text: But yeah — teamwork, right? Let's get into it.
From the back row, Casey watches, unaware of everything it took to get here.
He didn't have to say it like that. But he did.
Actually — a lot of this research is Maya's. The primary sources, the timeline logic, all of it.
The room goes quiet for half a second. Jordan blinks.
Right — yeah. I mean, she did do a lot of the — yeah. Maya found most of this.
Good to know. Go on.
Okay so — Maya, you want to take the next part?
...Sure. So this letter here is dated two weeks before the strike actually happened—
Jordan finishes strong, riding applause that isn't entirely his to take.
Thank you, thank you.

lines.action: says nothing, eyes down, gathering her notecards

lines.text: Nice work, all three of you. Take your seats.
Should've said something. Didn't. The silence is going to sit there a while.

lines.action: walks back to her seat, doesn't meet anyone's eyes

lines.text: The player catches Jordan's eye — a small tilt of the head toward Maya.
—and, uh. A lot of this research is actually Maya's. Just so that's clear.

lines.action: shoulders ease, just slightly, doesn't look up but almost smiles

lines.text: Anyway. Moving on.
Go on.
Not loud. Not perfect. But he said it.
The bell rings. The hallway floods.
Hey. Thank you. For back there.
You did the actual work. Seemed like the least anyone could do was say so.
Still. Thank you.
So. How'd it go? You've got a face like something happened.
Nico — a moment, please?
To be continued, apparently.
The hallway fills. Maya slips past without a word, eyes on the floor.
Maya—
So. How'd the presentation go?
It was... fine. Mostly.
That's not an answer.
Nico — a moment, please?
Saved by the bell. Sort of.
The hallway fills. Maya's walking a little taller than usual.
Thanks for going with me. To Ms. Patel. That was actually really hard.
You didn't have to do it alone, though. That was kind of the point.
Yeah. I noticed.
So. How'd the presentation go?
Good, actually.
...Huh. Okay. There's a story here and I'm getting it at lunch.
Ms. Patel's classroom, after the bell. She's cleaning her glasses, which means she's about to actually ask something.
So. How did the project really go? Who did what — was everyone's work represented honestly, in your opinion?

lines.action: waits, taps pen against her mug, doesn't fill the silence

lines.text: I just wanted to catch you before you head to lunch.
Am I in trouble?
No. I wanted to say I appreciated you and Maya coming to me when you did. It made grading a lot more straightforward. Full information, no guessing.
It felt kind of like a big deal at the time. Coming to you, I mean.
It was a big deal. That's exactly why it mattered.
Go on. You'll be late.

Honestly? We started with a shortcut — Jordan's brother's old template. We fixed most of it, but it started that way. And Maya did way more of the actual research than the first slide gave her credit for.
I appreciate that. I mean that — I know that wasn't the easy answer.
I'll grade with full information. That's all that means. Full information.
Didn't feel good, exactly. Felt clean, though. Clean's not nothing.
There were some rough patches. We figured most of it out along the way.
Rough patches. Okay.
lines.action: studies him a beat too long

lines.text: Good to know. Go on.
She knows there's more. She's just letting it go. For now.
It went great. Everyone contributed equally.
Good to hear.
lines.action: three full seconds of silence first

lines.text: Go on, you'll be late for lunch.
Those words sat heavier than they had any right to, all the way to the cafeteria.
Cafeteria noise. Trays. Somebody's dropped fork clatters three tables over.
So. You gonna tell me how it actually went, or should I just watch your face while you lie to me?
That obvious?
You've got a very readable face. It's kind of your whole thing.
Where do you even start with this one.

options.text: "Wait, I think Maya was onto something — let's actually hear it."
Say nothing and let the moment pass.

a.text: Wait, I think Maya was onto something — let's actually hear it.
Oh. Yeah, no, go for it.
It's just — the pull-tab thing is cool, but if we anchor it in actual letters from people who lived through it, it's not just flashy. It's real.

b.action: says nothing, taps two fingers against the desk
goes quiet, pulls her folder a little tighter

b.text: Okay so pull-tabs it is. I'll sketch it tonight.
Felt like a door closing somewhere. Not sure whose.

c.text: What if we combined both ideas? Pull-tabs, but they open up to the primary sources Maya found?
Ooh. Okay. That's actually — yeah, that could work.
That could actually work really well, structurally.
See, this is why we're a team. Kind of.

options.text: "No — let's just do the real work. I'll help."
"Fine, just this once. It's not a big deal."
"Let's use it as a structure, but cite everything properly."

a.text: No — let's just do the real work. I'll help. We've got two days, that's enough.
Two days is not a lot of time, man.
It's enough if we split it up right now.
a.emotion: quiet, then a little more sure

a.text: ...Fine. Fine! What do you need me to do.

b.text: Fine. Just this once. It's not that big a deal.
See, this is why you're my favorite group member.

b.action: doesn't argue, opens her folder to start swapping in facts

b.text: Didn't feel like a decision. Felt more like the path of least resistance.

c.text: Let's use it as a structure, but we cite everything properly and swap in our own research.
That's basically the same amount of work as starting over.
It's not, though. It's a shortcut on the boring parts, not the important ones.

options.text: "That's not okay. Let's fix it with Jordan, together."
"I'm sure it'll work out — try not to worry."
"I think we should talk to Ms. Patel about how this actually came together — will you come with me?"

a.text: That's not okay. Let's fix it — with Jordan, together. He should hear it from both of us.
You don't have to make it a whole thing.
It's already a whole thing. It's just a quiet one right now.

b.text: I'm sure it'll work out. Try not to worry about it too much.
Felt like the easy thing to say. Not sure it was the true thing.

c.text: I think we should talk to Ms. Patel about how this actually came together. Will you come with me?
Before's better than after. At least this way it's on our terms.
...Okay. Yeah. I don't want to do it alone, so — okay.

options.text: Speak up now: "Actually, Maya did most of this research."
Stay quiet and let the presentation finish.
Catch Jordan's eye and quietly gesture him toward crediting Maya himself.

a.text: Actually — a lot of this research is Maya's. The primary sources, the timeline, all of it.
I mean — yeah. Yeah, she did a lot of it.

a.action: shoulders drop, quiet exhale of relief

b.action: stays quiet, taps two fingers against his leg
finishes strong, takes a small bow

b.text: Had the moment right there. Let it go by.

c.action: catches Jordan's eye, tilts his head slightly toward Maya

c.text: —and, uh, a lot of this is actually Maya's research. Just want that clear.

options.text: Tell her everything, including the shortcut and the credit issue.
Mention "some rough patches" without naming names.

a.text: Honestly? We started with a shortcut — Jordan's brother's old template. We caught it and fixed most of it, but it started that way. And Maya did way more of the research than the first draft ever showed.
I appreciate that. I know that wasn't the easy answer.
I'll grade with full information. That's all that means.

b.text: There were some rough patches. We worked most of it out.

b.action: studies him a beat too long, taps pen against her mug

c.text: It went great, honestly. Everyone contributed equally.
Go on, you'll be late for lunch.

lines.text: Honestly? I told Ms. Patel everything — the shortcut, the credit thing, all of it. Even the parts that made us look bad.
That couldn't have been easy.
It wasn't. But Maya actually looked at me different after. Like — trusted me different.
That's worth more than a grade, probably.
Jordan's still a little weird about it. But he stopped taking sole credit mid-sentence now. Progress.
We fixed it. Quietly. Jordan doesn't even totally realize I pushed for it — he just thinks it was his idea to fix the credits slide.
So you managed him without him noticing. Impressive, honestly.
I don't know if 'managed' is the word.
It's the word.
Maya knows, though. That's the part that actually matters.
It went fine. Mostly fine.
Mostly.
I didn't really say anything, when I probably should have. A couple times.
Hm.
Maya's been kind of distant since. I don't think anything blew up. I just think I let some stuff slide that maybe mattered.
Yeah. That'll do that.
It went great. Grade's gonna be good.
You don't sound like someone with a good grade coming.
We used a shortcut. And Jordan took all the credit. And I told Ms. Patel everyone contributed equally, which — wasn't exactly true.
So it went great, except for the parts where it didn't.
Yeah. Except for those parts.
Maya know you know?
...I don't know. Probably.
Actually? Maya and I went to Ms. Patel before presentation day. Told her everything ourselves.
Before. Huh. Didn't see that coming.
Neither did I, kind of. But it meant presentation day wasn't this whole landmine.
So no last-minute chaos. That's new for you.
Turns out you can just... deal with things before they blow up. Wild concept.
Groundbreaking. Truly.

voiceNotes: Nico (player) hedges and softens ('Cool. Uh — cool.'), leans on humor to deflect discomfort, and his INTERNAL lines do the emotional labor his spoken lines avoid — he rarely says what he's actually feeling out loud. Jordan overtalks, especially when nervous or caught ('Right, right—'), and his defensiveness always arrives as arms-crossed deflection before it cracks into a short, quiet, genuine line ('...Yeah. No, you're right.'). Maya undersells everything she's proud of ('It's not a big deal, I just—') and her stillness/silence is doing more work than her words in any dismissal scene — she squares her notecards as a nervous ritual and it should show up whenever she's uneasy. Casey never asks a direct emotional question twice; they let 'hm' or silence pull the truth out instead, and their humor is dry, economical, one clause at a time. Ms. Patel's three-second pause after a question is a deliberate mannerism that should read as patient, not passive-aggressive — she genuinely means 'I appreciate that' every time she says it.

alternatives: Right, right, sources — we'll definitely use some.
Sure, sure, we'll work sources in somewhere.
alternativeLines.reasoning: Keeps Jordan's overtalking verbal tic ('Right, right—') consistent while varying delivery for repeat playthroughs.

alternatives: I just don't want to have done all this and have nobody know it was me.
I guess I just wanted it to matter that I did it.
alternativeLines.reasoning: Maya's line needs to land as vulnerable but not whiny — these alternates keep her precise, undersold register if the primary line feels too on-the-nose in playtesting.

alternatives: I don't think I lied, exactly. I just didn't say the true thing when it counted.
Nothing blew up. It just kind of... quietly didn't get fixed.
alternativeLines.reasoning: Offers a slightly less self-aware phrasing for players who want the conflict-avoider ending to feel more like denial than clear-eyed regret.
